articles.label: Article 5
articles.title: Governance & Organisation
articles.text: The management body of the financial entity shall define, approve, oversee and be responsible for the implementation of all arrangements related to the ICT risk management framework. The management body shall bear the ultimate responsibility for managing the entity's ICT risk, and shall define, approve and oversee the entity's digital operational resilience strategy. It shall allocate an appropriate budget to fulfil the entity's digital operational resilience needs and set appropriate risk tolerance levels for ICT risk.
risks: Board fails to formally own ICT risk, creating accountability gaps
Inadequate budget allocation leaves resilience needs unmet
Risk tolerance undefined, exposing entity to unmanaged ICT threats
Management body lacks ICT expertise to discharge oversight duties
Digital resilience strategy not reviewed annually, causing regulatory drift
controls: Board adopts a formal Digital Operational Resilience Strategy annually
Appoint a dedicated CISO with board-level reporting line
Embed ICT risk tolerance thresholds in the enterprise risk appetite statement
Ring-fence a dedicated ICT resilience budget approved by senior management
Conduct annual board-level training on DORA obligations

articles.label: Article 6
articles.title: ICT Risk Management Framework
articles.text: Financial entities shall have a sound, comprehensive and well-documented ICT risk management framework as part of their overall risk management system, which enables them to address ICT risk quickly, efficiently and comprehensively. Financial entities, other than microenterprises, shall assign the responsibility for managing and overseeing ICT risk to a control function and ensure an appropriate level of independence of such control function in order to avoid conflicts of interest. The ICT risk management framework shall be subject to internal audit by auditors on a regular basis.
risks: Absence of documented ICT risk framework exposes firm to regulatory censure
Conflicts of interest when risk and control functions share responsibilities
Outdated risk tolerance levels leave the firm blind to new threat vectors
Inadequate audit segregation increases operational exposure
Failure of periodic internal audit triggers supervisory investigation
controls: Publish a documented ICT Risk Management Framework with board sign-off
Establish three-lines-of-defence with independent ICT control function
Define and annually review risk and impact tolerance thresholds with KPIs
Schedule annual independent internal audits of the ICT risk framework
Implement a formal remediation process for critical audit findings

articles.label: Article 8
articles.title: Identification of ICT Risks
articles.text: Financial entities shall identify, classify and adequately document all ICT supported business functions, roles and responsibilities, the information assets and ICT assets supporting those functions, and their roles and dependencies in relation to ICT risk. Financial entities shall identify all sources of ICT risk, in particular the risk exposure to and from other financial entities, and assess relevant cyber threats and ICT vulnerabilities relevant to their ICT supported business functions, information assets and ICT assets.
risks: Unclassified ICT assets create blind spots in risk identification
Unknown interdependencies amplify impact of ICT failures
Failure to track third-party exposure leads to supply-chain threats
Missing asset register prevents accurate business impact assessments
Legacy systems introduce unidentified cyber vulnerabilities
controls: Maintain a comprehensive continuously updated ICT asset inventory
Map all business functions to supporting ICT assets and data flows
Conduct bi-annual threat and vulnerability assessments across critical systems
Implement automated discovery tools to detect unregistered ICT assets
Establish a formal process to track and evaluate third-party ICT dependencies

articles.label: Article 9
articles.title: Protection & Prevention
articles.text: Financial entities shall have in place appropriate and comprehensive ICT security policies, procedures, protocols and tools that aim to ensure the resilience, continuity and availability of ICT systems and to maintain high standards of availability, authenticity, integrity and confidentiality of data, whether at rest, in use or in transit. Financial entities shall design network infrastructure connections to be redundant and shall ensure that all identified risks are adequately mitigated.
risks: Inadequate encryption exposes data in transit to interception
Absence of network redundancy creates single points of failure
Weak access controls allow unauthorised access to sensitive assets
Outdated security policies fail to address emerging cyber threats
No integrity monitoring enables undetected tampering of critical data
controls: Implement end-to-end encryption for all data in transit and at rest
Deploy redundant network paths and geographically diverse data centres
Enforce role-based access control with privileged access management
Review and update ICT security policies at least annually
Deploy continuous integrity monitoring across all critical ICT systems

articles.label: Article 11
articles.title: Response & Recovery
articles.text: As part of their ICT risk management framework, financial entities shall put in place a comprehensive ICT business continuity policy, which may be adopted as a dedicated specific policy, forming an integral part of the overall business continuity policy of the financial entity. The policy shall address scenarios of severe business disruptions and aim to ensure that all necessary components are in place to allow the financial entity to effectively respond to and recover from ICT disruptions.
risks: Absence of a tested BCP leaves entity unable to recover from disruptions
Undocumented recovery scenarios result in ad hoc crisis responses
Failure to activate continuity plans exposes entity to prolonged outages
Untested RTOs may be unachievable under real conditions
No cross-functional ownership of continuity plans creates accountability gaps
controls: Develop and maintain a documented ICT Business Continuity Policy
Define and test RTOs and RPOs annually for all critical functions
Conduct full business continuity simulation exercises at least once per year
Assign continuity ownership to senior managers for each critical function
Integrate ICT continuity plans with the enterprise-wide BCP framework

articles.label: Article 12
articles.title: Backup & Recovery Policies
articles.text: Financial entities shall implement ICT backup policies and procedures specifying the scope of the data subject to the backup and the minimum frequency of the backup, based on the criticality of information or the confidentiality level of the data. Financial entities shall also implement ICT recovery procedures and recovery methods. When restoring backup data using own systems, financial entities shall use ICT systems that are physically and logically segregated from the source ICT system.
risks: Infrequent backups create large data loss windows in the event of failure
Backups stored on same systems risk simultaneous loss during an incident
Unverified backup integrity leads to failed recovery when needed most
No documented restoration procedures cause delays during crises
Ransomware may corrupt all backup copies if not segregated
controls: Define backup frequency based on data criticality with minimum thresholds
Store backups on physically and logically segregated immutable systems
Test backup restoration quarterly and document results with sign-off
Maintain written ICT recovery procedures accessible offline in crises
Implement air-gapped offsite backup copies for all critical data sets

articles.label: Article 17
articles.title: ICT Incident Management
articles.text: Financial entities shall define, establish and implement an ICT-related incident management process to detect, manage and notify ICT-related incidents. Financial entities shall record all ICT-related incidents and significant cyber threats. Financial entities shall put in place appropriate procedures and processes to ensure a consistent and integrated monitoring, handling and follow-up of ICT-related incidents, and to ensure that root causes are identified and addressed so as to prevent the recurrence of such incidents.
risks: Undetected ICT incidents escalate into major disruptions before containment
Inconsistent incident classification causes regulatory reporting failures
Lack of root cause analysis results in recurring incidents
Absence of incident logs prevents post-incident regulatory evidence
No formal escalation paths delay senior management notification
controls: Establish 24/7 ICT incident detection and monitoring with defined thresholds
Implement an incident classification taxonomy aligned with DORA categories
Enforce mandatory root cause analysis for all significant ICT incidents
Maintain tamper-proof incident logs for the minimum regulatory retention period
Define escalation matrices with response SLAs for each incident severity level

articles.label: Article 26
articles.title: Advanced Testing (TLPT)
articles.text: Financial entities identified by competent authorities shall carry out at least every 3 years advanced testing by means of threat-led penetration testing (TLPT). This testing shall cover several or all critical or important functions of a financial entity, and shall be performed on live production systems supporting such functions. Financial entities shall maintain full control over the entire TLPT process and shall ensure that appropriate risk mitigations are in place to avoid any adverse impact.
risks: Untested resilience leaves critical systems vulnerable to sophisticated actors
Failure to conduct TLPT every 3 years breaches DORA obligations
Testing on non-production systems fails to identify real-world vulnerabilities
Inadequate scope exclusions leave critical functions outside the test perimeter
Poor coordination with competent authorities invalidates TLPT outcomes
controls: Schedule TLPT exercises at least every 3 years using accredited external testers
Conduct testing on live production systems supporting all critical functions
Agree TLPT scope in advance with the relevant competent authority
Implement a formal remediation plan to address all findings within agreed timelines
Maintain comprehensive documentation of each TLPT exercise and mitigations

articles.label: Article 28
articles.title: Third-Party ICT Risk
articles.text: Financial entities shall manage ICT third-party risk as an integral component of ICT risk within their ICT risk management framework. Financial entities shall adopt and regularly review a strategy on ICT third-party risk, including a policy on the use of ICT services supporting critical or important functions provided by ICT third-party service providers. Financial entities shall maintain an up-to-date register of information in relation to all contractual arrangements on the use of ICT services.
risks: Undocumented ICT third-party arrangements create unmonitored risk exposure
Concentration risk from a single ICT provider threatens operational resilience
Failure to conduct due diligence allows non-resilient vendors into supply chain
Absence of an ICT third-party register breaches DORA reporting requirements
No exit strategy for critical ICT providers creates vendor lock-in risk
controls: Maintain a comprehensive up-to-date register of all ICT third-party arrangements
Establish a formal due diligence and onboarding process for all ICT providers
Assess and document concentration risk across all critical ICT providers annually
Develop and test exit strategies and transition plans for critical ICT providers
Include DORA-required audit and inspection rights in all ICT third-party contracts

articles.label: Article 30
articles.title: Key Contractual Provisions
articles.text: The rights and obligations of the financial entity and of the ICT third-party service provider shall be clearly allocated and set out in writing. Contractual arrangements shall include at minimum: a clear and complete description of all functions and ICT services to be provided; the locations where such services are to be provided; provisions on data portability and availability, return and secure deletion of data; service level descriptions including updates and revisions; relevant provisions on ICT security, including access rights, incident reporting and audit rights.
risks: Contracts lacking DORA clauses create unenforceable service obligations
Absent data portability provisions cause vendor lock-in post-exit
No defined service levels make it impossible to measure ICT resilience
Missing incident reporting clauses prevent timely notification of third-party breaches
Failure to include audit rights bars the entity from regulatory inspections
controls: Include full DORA-mandated clauses in all new and renewed ICT contracts
Mandate data portability, return and secure deletion provisions in all agreements
Define measurable SLAs covering availability, performance and security standards
Require ICT providers to notify the entity of incidents within agreed timeframes
Retain unrestricted audit and inspection rights over all critical ICT providers
